context.description: The text argues that bodily regulation, homeostasis, and survival needs shape most daily behavior. It claims this influence limits free will because the body quietly pushes behavior toward balance, with examples such as hunger, thirst, breathing, sleep, and waste elimination.
normalization_notes: No visible chapter heading or title on the page.
Source language is German; summary translated into English.

rows.paragraph_text: Bodily control systems usually operate in the background and become noticeable only when something goes wrong, such as the kidneys signaling a need for more oxygen in the blood or the brain registering low osmotic pressure and triggering thirst.
rows.text_content: Bodily control systems usually operate in the background and become noticeable only when something goes wrong.

rows.paragraph_text: Daily behavior is described as organized around maintaining balance and survival, with work and other activities ultimately serving the goal of keeping the body going.
rows.text_content: Daily behavior is organized around maintaining balance and survival.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage argues that this is not a matter of free choice: the body pushes behavior in directions it finds advantageous, and resistance can only delay the need to comply.
rows.text_content: The body pushes behavior in directions it finds advantageous, limiting free will.

rows.paragraph_text: Examples illustrate that ignoring bodily needs leads to immediate physical consequences: breath-holding, dehydration, not using the toilet, exposure to cold, sleep deprivation, starvation, or unsafe desert travel.
rows.text_content: Ignoring bodily needs leads to immediate physical consequences.

rows.paragraph_text: The final visible line begins a further example about how little direct control we have over bodily systems, such as deciding to run the heart permanently at 30 beats per minute.
rows.text_content: The passage begins another example of limited control over bodily systems.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness German philosophy of consciousness page on homeostasis, survival, and the limits of free will. Body regulation, thirst, hunger, sleep, and other needs are presented as forces that shape daily behavior.
